one_line_takeaway: MOX reframes LLM unlearning as 'memorize then negate': train the forget set harder with plain gradient descent + a retain-set KL constraint, then subtract that memorization direction via tunable task-vector extrapolation (theta_for=(1+alpha)theta_ref-alpha*theta_mem), sidestepping gradient-ascent collapse and preserving utility, though absolute forget quality still trails true retraining.
key_finding: MOX matches or beats a broad set of unlearning baselines on forget quality while preserving utility and, per the authors, avoiding GA's instability. On TOFU/Llama2-7B, MOX(momentum) reaches FQ=0.0680 and MU=0.6528 (best or near-best MU among all methods), versus NPO FQ=0.0068/MU=0.6321 and GA FQ=0.0143/MU=0.6333; increasing alpha monotonically raises forgetting (FQ 0.0146 at alpha=0.5 up to 0.0625 at alpha=4.0, dropping to 0.0319 at alpha=8.0 as utility erodes). On MUSE/Llama2-7B, MOX drives VerbMem/KnowMem on D_F down (momentum: 0.2 / 0.8) while keeping KnowMem on D_R at 54.8 (near the Retain-model 55.0 and far above collapsed GA/NPO which hit 0.0/0.0/0.0). Extrapolation is nearly free: GD training is 43 TFLOPs/sample (same as NPO), the extrapolation step is only 21 GFLOPs, computing 5 alpha-variant models takes 4.7s, and a momentum update 2.5s.
core_contribution: The paper proposes MOX (MOdel eXtrapolation), a machine-unlearning method for LLMs that removes undesired knowledge WITHOUT gradient ascent (GA). The authors' own diagnosis is that GA, the standard way to reverse training on a forget set, causes 'catastrophic collapse' and utility loss regardless of loss reweighting. Their counter-strategy: use ordinary gradient descent (GD) to intensify memorization of the forget set D_F (with a KL-divergence regularizer keeping predictions on the retain set D_R consistent with the reference model), yielding a 'memorization model' theta_mem that is the dual/opposite of the desired forget model. The forget model is then obtained purely by parameter arithmetic: theta_for = (1+alpha)*theta_ref - alpha*theta_mem, where alpha tunes forgetting strength. This builds on task-arithmetic negation from Ilharco et al. [37] (cited prior work, NOT the paper's own idea) but extends it with a tunable extrapolation coefficient, a retain-set KL constraint, a targeted-unlearning loss variant, and a momentum-ensembling variant (eta=0.675). The paper also states an 'irreversible gradient' criterion (Def 2.1) and Theorem 2.4 claiming GD-only optimization converges without collapse under a PL/L-smooth assumption.
threat_model: Legitimate safety/privacy motivation stated by the paper: LLMs trained on large-scale data memorize and can leak private, copyrighted, or malicious/harmful knowledge (they cite GDPR/CCPA, copyright violations, and WMDP-style malicious-use concerns). The goal is post-hoc removal of a designated forget set from a deployed model without full retraining (which is prohibitively expensive) and without degrading general utility. This is a data-governance / knowledge-removal setting rather than an adversarial-attacker setting; the paper does not model an active adversary crafting inputs, and does not claim robustness against relearning/extraction attacks (it only cites benign-relearning work [64] in related work, not as an evaluation).
limitations: 1) Absolute forget quality remains weak: MOX's best TOFU FQ (~0.068) is two orders of magnitude below the Retrained-LLM reference (FQ=1.000), so 'effective forgetting' is relative to weak baselines, not to true retraining. 2) On MUSE, no MOX configuration lands inside the ideal PrivLeak band [-5%,5%] (closest are alpha=4 at -19.8 and momentum at -18.4; alpha=8 overshoots to +35.8), so the 'avoids privacy leakage' claim is only directional. 3) alpha is a sensitive, dataset-specific knob (best alpha differs between TOFU and MUSE) requiring tuning against ground-truth-style evaluation. 4) Memory cost is 3x theta_ref versus 2x for NPO (mitigated only by offloading extrapolation to CPU). 5) No evaluation against relearning/extraction/membership-inference attacks, so robustness of the forgetting is untested. 6) The convergence-without-collapse theorem rests on a PL-condition assumption whose validity for real LLMs is not empirically established (proof deferred to appendix). 7) Reported gains over the strongest utility-preserving baselines (SimNPO, RMU, AltPO) are often small.

datasets_used: TOFU benchmark (200 fictitious author profiles, 20 QA pairs each; Forget/Retain/Real Authors/Real World splits; forget ratios 1%/5%/10%)
MUSE benchmark (News corpus of BBC news collected after Aug 2023)
quantitative_results: TOFU/Llama2-7B MOX(momentum): FQ=0.0680, MU=0.6528, F-RL=0.4410, R-RL=0.9802
TOFU/Llama2-7B reference points: Original LLM FQ=0.0000/MU=0.6346; Retrained LLM FQ=1.0000/MU=0.6267; GA FQ=0.0143/MU=0.6333; NPO FQ=0.0068/MU=0.6321
TOFU/Phi-1.5B MOX(momentum): FQ=0.0598, MU=0.5510, F-RL=0.3120, R-RL=0.8988
MUSE/Llama2-7B MOX(momentum): VerbMem D_F=0.2, KnowMem D_F=0.8, KnowMem D_R=54.8, PrivLeak=-18.4
MUSE reference points: Retain model 20.8/33.1/55.0/0.0; GA and NPO collapse to 0.0/0.0/0.0 on D_R utility
Efficiency: GD training 43 TFLOPs/sample (equal to NPO); extrapolation step 21 GFLOPs; 5 alpha-variant models in 4.7s; momentum update 2.5s; memory 3x theta_ref (NPO 2x)
